spqr_p.bio:
Titus Pullo comes from an old patrician Roman family, which unfortunately for the Senate means he technically belongs among them. Raised with tutors, family expectations, and noble manners, Pullo ignored most of it and preferred soldiers, gambling, wine, and arguments settled with fists.

By 218 BC, with Hannibal crossing into Italy, Pullo has found himself in the Senate — partly because of his bloodline, partly because of family influence, and partly because nobody managed to stop it in time. He has political ambition, but not much patience for elegant speeches. His idea of diplomacy is shouting louder than the other man.

Despite his rough behavior, Pullo is loyal to Rome and deeply popular with common soldiers. He speaks like a man of the people, drinks like one, and somehow votes like a noble when he remembers to attend. Other senators consider him crude, dangerous, and impossible to control.

**In short:** a patrician senator with the manners of a legionary and the survival instincts of a drunk mule.

spqr_p.bio:
Quintus Velcha is a plebeian senator from the coast of Etruria, born into a small mercantile family that owned a modest fishery. Though far removed from the ancient patrician dynasties of Rome, the Velcha family gradually earned respectable wealth and standing through honest trade, supplying fish, salt and preserved goods to nearby ports and Roman markets.

As a young man, Quintus spent much of his life amongst fishing vessels, merchant ships and harbours across the Republic, granting him a deep understanding of the sea and maritime trade. During periods of piracy and unrest, he served alongside Roman naval patrols escorting merchant shipments and grain convoys operating between Sicily and Rome.

Seeking greater honour and advancement, Quintus eventually entered public life and military service. He later served with Roman forces in northern Italia during the campaign against Hannibal and witnessed the disaster at Trebia firsthand, an experience that still weighs heavily upon him.

Now seeking the office of Praefectus Classis, Quintus advocates strengthening the Roman fleet, securing Sicily and protecting the Republic’s maritime lifelines against Carthaginian influence.

Amongst the Senate he is often referred to simply as “the Etrurian,” a title he wears with quiet pride.

spqr_p.bio:
Born into the gens Picia, a Patrician family of modest distinction, Gaius Pictus Perictus built his reputation not through battlefield glory but through the quieter, harder work of diplomacy and law. Where others sought honor in the legions, Perictus spent his years in the courts, in treaty negotiations, and among the allied peoples of Italy — learning their languages, their grievances, and their pride.
He is not a man of grand gestures. He is a man of patient, precise action.
His alliance with Gaius Flaminius surprised many in the Senate. A Patrician standing beside a Plebian reformer was not without risk to his standing among the old families. Perictus accepted that risk without hesitation — because he saw in Flaminius not a demagogue, but a practical Roman who got things done. The Via Flaminia did not build itself through noble birth. Italy was not pacified through ancestry alone.
That conviction sits at the heart of who Perictus is. He believes the Republic is strongest when its offices are filled by those most capable of holding them — regardless of the family name attached.

spqr_p.bio: I was born into the ancient Patrician gens Valeria, a house long associated with discipline, civiv duty, and stern Republican virtue. My father served with distinction in the later stages of the First Punic War, and instilled in me a lifelong belief that the greatness of Rome rested on endurance and discipline. I have studied various military histories as opposed to racing or gambling, and served as a military tribune in Cisalpine Gaul, now I stand in the Senate, doing my utmost to preserve our Republic from internal and external factors. I believe in bold decisive action, but only after the ground has been set in stone; I favor attention to the Plebians, as they are the foundations of the state; and I wish with all my heart to see Rome expand and spread hither her greatness and her laws.

spqr_p.bio:
Quintus Ulpius Silanus, first Senator of his family name.

The Ulpia have their roots in the Umbria region of Italia, made of small landowners in the area and had access to horses, forming a part of the Cavalry forces during the first Punic war, hence their status in the Equites.

Quintus himself got his position through a Military Tribuneship, followed by a Quaestorship, expending much of his inherited wealth in the process. He is evidently ambitious and aims to make a name for himself and his family.

spqr_m.body:
In the aftermath of the disaster at Trebia, the Republic must act swiftly to restore Roman military strength and reassure both our allies and citizens that Rome remains capable of defending Italy.

Therefore, this motion authorizes the immediate raising of Legio VI, Legio VII, Legio VIII and Legio IX alongside two additional cavalry units for the defense of the Republic.

This measure will provide Rome with an additional 20,000 legionaries and 4,000 cavalry, restoring losses suffered in recent campaigns and strengthening our ability to confront Hannibal Barca and any further threats to the Republic.

The total cost of this measure is 2,440T and 1,320M. The Republic currently possesses stockpiles of 5,390T and 4,505M, meaning Rome will retain substantial reserves even after recruitment is completed.

These reserves exist for moments such as this.

What purpose does a treasury serve if not to defend the Republic after disaster? What purpose does stored food serve if not to feed soldiers defending Italy?

Those who refuse to support the rebuilding of Rome’s armies after Trebia weaken the Republic at the very moment strength and unity are required most.

The Senate is therefore urged to approve this motion without delay so that recruitment, training and preparation may begin immediately.
spqr_m.title: Motion for the Raising of Legio VI, Legio VII, Legio VIII, Legio IX and Two Cavalry Units

spqr_m.body:
*Gaius Pictus Perictus rises before the Senate*

Patres Conscripti,

I come before you bearing news from the north, and I ask for silence, as it is of grave importance.

*Perictus waits until a palpable sense of tension fills the room, then continues*

More than three weeks ago, a rider left Cremona. He travelled not on open roads, for those belong to Hannibal's patrols and his Gallic brigands now, but through fields, through forests, evading them as they moved north to join the enemy's growing host. Today he arrived in Rome.

Here is what he told me.

Cremona holds. But it holds barely, a cohort of Hastati, a cohort of Principes, militia, stragglers, and four hundred Gallic auxiliaries whose loyalty, I shall be honest with you, is untested. These men stand as a garrison praying that the Senators of Rome remember they exist.

Meanwhile Hannibal sits at Placentia with an ever growing army, as numerous as fifty thousand. His cavalry outnumbered ours at Trebia by double. He also possesses elephants, the number disputed, but the certainty of their presence a fact. And around him, every day, more Gauls arrive. Not because he conquered them, but because he invited them.

Hannibal burns Roman farmsteads and leaves Gallic hovels standing. This message requires no translator.

He is not merely a general. He is a political act. Every farm he burns, every prisoner he releases, every tribe he welcomes is more than equal to a speech to the peoples of Italy about what Rome is and what Rome is not.

Which brings me to this.

When others in this chamber were still debating procedure, still discovering constitutional objections, still performing caution for the benefit of their reputations. Gaius Flaminius was already doing what Rome required. He saw Trebia for what it was. He saw the empty roads north and understood what filled them. He acted. He moved this Senate toward raising the legions that Cremona, that Patavium, that every Roman settlement throughout the valley of the River Padus is now desperately waiting for.

Today I speak plainly.

Cremona needs to know Rome is coming. Northern Italy needs to know Rome has not abandoned it. And Hannibal needs to know that the Senate on whose indecisiveness he counted has instead found its resolve.

I move that this body treat the situation in the north as the true military emergency it is, that both positions of Consul are filled without any further delay, and that envoys be dispatched immediately to Cremona, Patavium and Luna, so that the men on their walls know that Rome has heard them.

And that Rome will come to their aid, and to the aid of any other settlement that calls upon her!
spqr_m.title: Immediate Consul elections and the dispatching of envoys to Cremona, Patavium and Luna
spqr_m.resultNote: Automatically passed by majority: AYE 20 reached 20 required votes.

spqr_m.body:
*Aulus Domitius Ahenobarbus rises from his seat and waits for the chamber to quiet down*

"Conscript Fathers honoured Colleagues,

The sanctity of Rome and of this very Council must not be violated and any opportunity for this should be fought. Both the danger from within as well as those from outside. For this I propose the following Law"

No formation of 8 Men or above outside the City Cohort may they be private or military shall be allowed to come into the city limits of Roma under arms. In the event of direct danger to Roma a decision by both Consuls or the Dictator may suspend this law for up to 6 Months or until a Vote of the Senate overturns this decision.

Exemptions to this Rule shall be restricted to Votes by the full Senate and only in the Case of Official Triumphs or Festivitys.

Those who break this Law shall be punished by either banishment and confiscation of their property or be put to death so their family can be freed of the disgrace.

"I propose this solely for the benefit and security of our noble city and our Government."
spqr_m.title: Securitas Romae

spqr_m.body:
Lucius Valerius Corvinus rises before the senate: 
"Patres Conscripti!
At the Trebia, more Romans were in fact defeated by disorder and exposure than by Carthaginian steel, ordered to march through freezing water to their deaths by glory-seeking generals in the field. Therefore I propose that no Roman army operating beyond Latium shall march to battle in winter without first establishing a fortified camp, securing supply, and allowing proper rest to the men. Furthermore, let two military tribunes from each legion bear witness before the quaestors that these conditions have been fulfilled. Any commander found violating this decree through negligence or reckless haste shall answer before the Senate upon conclusion of his command. Let no man place haste or glory before discipline. 
I propose this for the sole purpose of safeguarding the security of our Republic and its noble Legions, and I hereby submit this motion to the Senate and People of Rome."
spqr_m.title: De Castris Hiemalibus (On Winter Camps)

spqr_m.body:
I propose the establishment of an official body, or corps, of scouts, mounted messengers, and informants attached to every consular army of the Republic. 
Let each consul appoint officers responsible sorely for: 
1, The reconnaissance of roads, rivers, forests, and terrain
2, The tracking of enemy movements
3, The questioning of travelers, merchants, and prisoners, 
4, The regular dispatch of reports to both commanding generals and the Senate

Furthermore, let relay stations and mounted couriers be organized along the principle roads of Italy so that news of movement or battle may swiftly reach Rome and her allies. No commander again shall claim ignorance before risking the lives of Roman citizens and allies.
spqr_m.title: De Officio Exploratorum (On the Office of Scouts of Intelligence)
spqr_m.speech:
Patres Conscripti, 
Too often in this war the armies of Rome march uncertain of the enemy's position, strength, and intentions, while the Carthaginian moves with certainty, knowledge, and preparation. Therefore I propose the establishment of an official body, or corps, of scouts, mounted messengers, and informants attached to every consular army of the Republic, and relay stations and mounted couriers be organized along the principle roads of Italy for faster news transfer. 

Some of you may point out the obvious existance of our exploratores, our patrols, our guides and messengers. But I state to you all they are ad hoc, inconsistent, dependent on the individual commander, and often badly coordinated. We still think in terms of courage, discipline, and the fire of direct battle while Hannibal humiliates us through intelligence, concealment, and maneuver. What I wish is to take what we have, and systematically organize and professionalize the gathering of information so as to enable better decisions and results in the field.

If any of you would question the cost, I say to you that they are definitively small. Most of the work is reallocation and organization. It is a relatively small matter to assign 100 dedicated mounted scouts, relay stations, intelligence officers, and conduct informant payments. It is minimal compared to raising a new legion, or replacing our losses at Trebia! It has a high strategic return for a low financial burden. If the Republic already spends vast treasure to replace destroyed armies, let us spend a little instead to prevent their destruction. Otherwise do we raise legions for their eventual destruction? 

If any senator present has objections, raise them. I shall be delighted to defend on the Senate floor. I sincerely believe it is through small steps like these that our strength grows.
spqr_m.effects:
My motion, if passed, would enable: 
1, Earlier warning of Hannibal's movements. Better reconnaissance, faster intelligence flow, less confusion, and quicker concentration of our forces. 
2, Better terrain awareness. So that our commanders do not blunder into ambushes, rivers, poor ground, and hidden enemy positions. 
3, Reduced Consular Recklessness. An added touch of restraint and security. 
4, Stronger coordination across Italy. A dramatic improvement in operational coherence. Rome is powerful, yet its strength is scattered. Its strength would increase vastly once coordination is learned. 
5, More accurate Senate awareness. Instead of relying on confused and panic-invoking rumors, we rely on regular dispatches and standardized reports.

spqr_m.body: I move to enact an immediate, emergency moratorium on all debts, principal obligations, and accumulating interest held by any citizen or Italian ally currently enrolled in the legions of Rome.  Furthermore, all foreclosure actions, seizures of agricultural property, and legal penalties against the families of active soldiers shall be suspended for the duration of the war against Carthage, until such a time as the Republic is declared secure.
spqr_m.title: De Nexo et Usuris Militum
spqr_m.speech: As I promised in my bid for the Consulship, I declare before the Senate for its approval the halting of all duties of interest, debt, and legal penalties on our soldiers.  Our soldiers must not be worried for the financial security of themselves and their families while they are fighting for the survival of our Republic.  Our soldiers leave behind their plows, their small farms, their wives and children to fight, they deserve whatever protections we can give.  Let the moneylenders wait for their gold until our soldiers have finished saving Rome!
spqr_m.effects: Prevent the growth of wealth inequalities in the countryside by preventing the soldiery from being saddled by debt and interest.

spqr_m.body:
The Senate is currently deadlocked due to a poor will amongst Senators to act on their requirement to vote on bills, leading to motions stalling flat on the floor.

This Proposal is that only one third of the Senate voting will be required for the motion to pass in the event a Majority of Senators have failed to vote during a time of war or crisis.

Once victory is had, it is intended for this to be withdrawn in peace.
spqr_m.title: Propositio de Flexibilitate Senatoria - A Proposal for Senatorial Flexibility
spqr_m.speech:
Abruptly, Quintus Ulpius Silanus - one of the youngest Senators currently enrolled - stands, drawing a roll of parchment from his Toga as he now takes the floor.

"Patres Conscripti!"

He booms now, frustration visible, gesturing to his fellow Senators sitting towards the back with the parchment before he begins to speak, bordering on frothing at the mouth.

"This Senate is in deadlock and turmoil, many Senators have failed in their honourable duty to vote here, in a time of great peril, when the Carthaginian Barbarian knocks on the door of Italia, of ROME! Many fail to vote on critical motions to assist in Rome's defence."

The Equestrian now walks, circling the floor like a wolf on the edge of a clearing,
"Will we continue to stand idly by and let the idlers in this Senate drag the feet of the Republic in its most turbulent hour? No! We will do without, this motion will have it be so that only a third of this body will be required to vote on a motion for it to be able to be passed into law, in the case that less than a Majority of this body has cast their votes."

"I hope this motion is able to pass succinctly, and will enable the Republic to react efficiently and swiftly. I also hope, it'll allow those standing idly by as the Republic burns to find their voices! Lest they be as still as stone, and let Rome become but ashen waste in face of the Barbarian, Barca!"

He now gestures on to the door of Senate house, in direction of the forum with two fingers of his right hand as the roll hangs by his side in his left,
"I implore this to be voted on sooner rather than later, less more drastic measures be required. The citizenry calls for action, and I intend to have this Senate heed it!"

He booms finally, as he now moves to return to his seat.
spqr_m.effects: This motion when passed would enable the Senate to act despite a high proportion of the body being inactive and failing to vote on motions, especially now that motions can be thrown out if they aren't voted upon within a specific timeframe.

spqr_o.text:
Legio I, along with the Equites Consulares and Socii Equites 2, to march from Latium to Arretium, combining them with Legio II and Socii Equites 1.  

Scouts and messengers will be dispersed  throughout Etruria to have advanced warning of any enemy movement.  Socii in Etruria will be given orders to consolidate all food they can into fortified towns, and Legio II should send garrisons throughout Etruria to reinforce these fortifications.   Any food that cannot be fortified in towns should be burned.  Additionally, send word to the Socii  in Etruria that any who join the Legions for 5 years or until the end of this war (whichever comes first), will be granted full Roman Citizenship.  This has the guarantee of a Consul. 

If Hannibal moves through the swamps of Northern etruria (the swampy Arno river valley), prepare to quickly attack him at the expected exit-point of the swamp when his troops are tired and sick.  If any other movements of Hannibal are detected they are to be reported ASAP so I can decide whether we shall counter them.

Legio VI in Latium, Legio VII in Corfinium, and Legio VIII in Etruria will be dispatched to Arretium as soon as they have finished their muster.

Early Summer · 218 BC · Turn II

Following the disaster at Trebia, I hereby order the reorganisation and deployment of the Roman fleet into three operational commands in order to secure Italia, maintain maritime dominance, and prevent Punic naval interference within Roman waters.

Classis Etruria

Composition: 30 Triremes
Base of Operations: Ostia

Orders:
- Patrol the Tyrrhenian Sea and the western coastline of Italia
- Protect merchant shipping and maritime traffic moving toward Ostia
- Monitor for Punic raiding activity near central Italia
- Maintain rapid response readiness in the event of coastal raids or enemy naval sightings

Classis Magna Graecia

Composition: 20 Triremes
Base of Operations: Tarentum

- Patrol the waters of southern Italia and Magna Graecia
- Protect allied ports, settlements and maritime trade
- Monitor Punic naval movement within southern waters

Classis Sicilia

Composition: 30 Triremes
Base of Operations: Messana

- Secure Sicily and Rome’s supply and maritime trade routes
- Maintain Roman naval dominance around Sicily
- Intercept or shadow hostile Punic vessels where possible
- Ensure continued safe transport of food and supplies to the Republic

Intended Outcome

The objective of these deployments is to ensure:

- Continued Roman control of the seas
- Protection of vital grain and trade routes
- Security of allied coastal settlements
- Strategic isolation of Hannibal’s forces within Italia

Signed,
Quintus Velcha of Etruria
Praefectus Classis

Orders of Marcus Teutonicus Berolinensis, Praefectus Urbanus

In light of the loss of several northern cities, the movement of the Consuls toward the north, and the raising of new legions throughout Italy, the following measures are ordered within the city of Rome:

1. Additional watches shall be placed at the Forum, grain markets, city gates, river landings, temples and other places of public assembly. Their duty is to preserve order, prevent riot, report agitation, and bring offenders before the urban courts.

2. Reports from the north shall be collected through magistrates, merchants, refugees and private correspondents. False reports knowingly spread to cause panic, slander allies, or weaken public confidence shall be investigated and punished.

3.All significant grain stores within Rome shall be declared to the office of the Praefectus Urbanus. Honest merchants shall be protected, but hoarding, false scarcity and unlawful price manipulation in time of war shall be prosecuted.

4. Disputes concerning grain, transport, army supply, contracts and emergency purchases shall be heard swiftly, so that legal delay does not obstruct the provisioning of Rome or the legions.

5. Refugees from the northern settlements shall be counted, questioned and recorded. Their place of origin, route of travel, and reports of enemy movement shall be gathered. Those in genuine need shall be directed toward temporary shelter and assistance. For that I myself provide 20M of food to those in need.

6. Men claiming to be soldiers, deserters or survivors of defeated forces shall be separated from civilian refugees and examined. Useful military information shall be passed to the Consuls; cowardice or desertion shall be dealt with according to law.

7. Any attempt to evade the levy through bribery, false declarations, concealment of age, status, property or household obligation shall be investigated. Rome shall not permit the burden of war to fall only upon those too poor to buy evasion.

8. Allied envoys, merchants and petitioners within Rome shall be received promptly and treated with dignity, but their statements and loyalties shall be recorded. Loyal allies are to be reassured; doubtful men are to be watched.

The purpose of these measures is simple: while the Consuls march north, Rome herself must remain orderly, supplied, informed and unshaken.
spqr_o.resolution:
With the Consuls departed northward and the sound of distant catastrophe still ringing in the ears of the city, the Praetor Urbanus Marcus Teutonicus Berolinensis moves swiftly through the corridors of the Regia and into the streets of a restless capital. He is not a Consul. He does not command legions. But he holds the city — and in this moment, that is everything.

His edict is posted at the Rostra and the city gates within the day.
The watch rosters swell. New pairs of vigiles appear at the Forum Boarium and the Tiber landings before nightfall. At the Capena Gate and the Via Flaminia, scrutineers now question travelers from the north — separating the panicked from the merely displaced, and the disgraced from the merely unlucky. Refugees begin arriving in greater numbers now: families from Cisalpine settlements, Gallic traders who chose Rome's side and have paid for it, merchants who lost everything when Hannibal's outriders appeared on the horizon.

They are counted. They are questioned. Their knowledge of roads, troop positions, and Carthaginian movements is carefully extracted by clerks under the Praetor's authority and forwarded in sealed dispatches toward the Consuls.

Suspected deserters — men who claim to be survivors, whose stories do not align, whose equipment is missing or suspiciously clean — are separated and held in the Lautumiae pending formal examination. Three such men are already under questioning.

At the lawcourts near the Basilica, the Praetor's clerks announce accelerated hearings for disputes concerning supply contracts, transport, and military provisioning. Delay in these matters will not be tolerated while legions wait for grain and leather.

Meanwhile, the Praetor himself personally arranges for twenty measures of food from his own estate to be distributed at the Subura refugee shelter, an act that draws quiet murmurs of approval from the common people.

The grain declaration measure, however, is met with polite but firm resistance. The merchants' guild reminds the Praetor's secretary that grain market oversight belongs to the Aedile. Two merchants of some standing have already quietly noted they will comply with no such declaration unless the Aedile — or the Senate — instructs it. The Praetor's clerks stand down on this point without incident, though the matter is noted for follow-up.

spqr_o.text: To ensure that  those with the means to contribute more than others will truly aid Rome in her time of need, olive estates will pay double the standard tax rate.
spqr_o.resolution:
The intent is sound. The instinct is Roman. In a time of crisis, those who hold wealth in land and oil should bear a heavier share of the burden — this argument has been made before in the Senate, and it will be made again. But the Quaestor of Rome is not the Senate.

The Quaestor holds authority over the collection, disbursement, and accounting of public funds. He may audit. He may pay. He may refuse illegal expenditure. He may flag irregularities and report to the Senate. What he may not do is unilaterally alter the rate at which Roman citizens and Latin allies are taxed on their property.
Tax rates particularly those singling out a specific class of agricultural property are matters of law. They must be proposed before the Senate as a formal motion, debated, and voted upon. Alternatively, they may be introduced as a bill before the relevant assembly. Either way, a Quaestor acting alone has no constitutional authority to issue such a directive.

Were Spurius Papinianus Rufus to simply begin collecting double taxation from olive estate holders on his own authority, he would face immediate legal challenge. The affected landowners  many of them equestrians or senators of substance would appeal to the Tribunes or the Praetors within the week. The order would be struck down, the Quaestor embarrassed, and public confidence in the treasury's legitimacy damaged at precisely the moment Rome can least afford it.

The clerks of the aerarium receive the order, note it carefully, and mark it pending Senate authorization.

spqr_o.text:
Emergency Military Levy

Goal:
Rebuild the army after the defeat at Trebia, provide support for the poor, and lower the cost of raising Roman Legions by reducing the property requirement for military service.
By lowering the census requirement, Rome can increase the number of citizens eligible for service and raise more Roman Legions at a lower cost. If this is not enough, the reform may also allow the creation of cheaper Levy Legions as an emergency measure.

Step 1: The Public Assembly (Contio) – Rallying the Plebs
Action:
I will summon a large public meeting, a contio, in the Forum.
Rhetoric and Propaganda:
I will address the crowd and present the disaster at Trebia not as a failure of Roman courage, but as a failure of poor aristocratic command. I will declare that the survival of Rome now rests in the hands of the entire Roman people, the Populus Romanus.
Optimate Alignment:
I will argue that true Roman patriotism and public welfare lie in defending our homes, our patria, and that every citizen, regardless of wealth, has the sacred and equal right to fight and win glory for Rome.

Proposed Law:
Plebiscitum de Militia Proletariorum
The Reform:
This law temporarily lowers the minimum property requirement for service in the heavy infantry legions.
The Levy:
We will immediately enlist poorer citizens, including the proletarii, to replace our military losses.
Equipment:
The state and wealthy patrons will help pay for their weapons and armor.

Expected In-Game Outcomes:
Military:
Rome quickly gains thousands of new heavy infantry soldiers to fight Hannibal. By lowering the census requirement, the Republic can raise more Roman Legions at a reduced cost. If necessary, Rome may also form cheaper Levy Legions as an emergency response to the crisis.
Social:
The poor receive a stable income, stipendium, food, and a share of future war plunder.
Political:
This increases the popularity of the Optimates among the common people.
spqr_o.resolution:
The tribes vote. The urban tribes carry it. Two rural tribes — their clients pressured by conservative patrons vote against. The plebiscitum passes narrowly.

Tribune Gaius Servilius Structus declines to veto, but publicly frames his restraint as a reluctant wartime exception, not an endorsement. The conservative bloc loses the vote and immediately begins looking for ways to strangle implementation.
The law is Roman precedent. The rolls open. Men register.

Then nothing moves because nobody funded the equipment.

spqr_o.text: Take legio IV from Ancona to Arretium and join with legio III in Ariminum,the 2 legions will be stationed inside the town.Establish a supply depot at Ancona.Send spies to Cisalpiine Gaul to know about Hannibal's whereabouts. Establish roadblocks 50 m front of the Town of Arretium.Legio IX will be recruited from the Marsii.request the inhabitants of Picentum and Umbria to bring themselves,their grains and livestock into fortified posts.the troops under me shall be drilling for 3 hours per day while in camp.

With the power granted to the office of Aedile Curule, and the preservation of the Roman people during the war against Carthage and the Hannibal scourge. The Following orders will be enacted in service of the Republic.

1. Repair of Military Roads.

All roads leading to the military staging grounds, supply depots and recruitment centers shall be repaired with out delay. 

Local property owners and magistrates adjoining these roads are commanded to provide labor, stone, and the tools necessary for the completion of this work. Objections or refusal during this time of war shall incur fines levied by the office of Aedile.

2. Maintenance of Bridges and Aqueducts

All bridges vital to the movement of Roman legions and supply trains shall receive immediate inspection and repairs as needed. Aqueducts serving military encampments an districts essential to wartime production shall be maintained with highest priority. Engineers and laborers are hereby requistitioned in the service of the Republic until these works are secured.

3. Wartime Grain Decree

To ensure adequate grain reached the legions and mouths of the Roman people during this hour of danger, the state grain price shall be raised so that wealthy landowners and merchants are encouraged to release private stores into public commerce.

Furthermore, Senators, magistrates and persons of exception wealth of influence shall face restrictions upon the amount of grain they may purchase from state reserves, so that the common people and soliders do not suffer while the privileged hoard abundance.

Rome shall endure through shared sacrifice, not selfish excess.  

Let every citizen remember: walls and armies alone do not preserve Rome. Discipline, Duty, and Unity preserve Rome.

For the People!
For the Republic!
For Rome!

Vox Populi Romani!
spqr_o.resolution:
Nero Avitus Corvus moves fast for a new magistrate. His edicts appear on the walls of the Forum before the morning crowd has fully assembled, written in the direct language of a man who believes urgency is its own justification.

On the roads and bridges: Within Rome and the immediate approaches to the city, his order carries force. Crews are organized. Stone is sourced. The Via Appia's worst damaged sections near the city gates receive attention within days — a visible, practical improvement that soldiers and supply carts will feel immediately. Bridge inspectors are dispatched to crossings on the Tiber within the city's reach.

But when the Aedile's messengers ride out toward the distant staging grounds and supply depots of the north, they return with polite but firm responses from local magistrates and landowners: the Aedile's writ runs in Rome. Out here, they answer to the Consuls, or to the Senate. No fines are paid. No labor is compelled. The order simply stops at the edge of the Aedile's legal reach.

On the grain decree: This lands with immediate effect and immediate controversy.
The state grain price is raised. Within two days, private merchants and landowners — calculating that state grain is now priced attractively enough to cover their own costs — begin releasing stored grain into public commerce. The market loosens slightly. Supply improves marginally. The Aedile's logic is sound: price signals work.
The restriction on wealthy purchasers from state reserves is posted publicly. It is received with fury in the senatorial quarter and with quiet approval in the Subura.
 
Three equestrian merchants immediately dispatch letters of complaint to sympathetic senators. Two senators send private messages to the Aedile questioning his authority to restrict their access to public markets. The Aedile is legally on solid ground — but he has made enemies before his office chair is warm.

spqr_laws.b: The foundation of Roman law. Citizens may not be punished without trial. All citizens are equal before these written laws. Justice must be seen, not merely administered in darkness.
spqr_laws.t: Lex Duodecim Tabularum (451 BC) — The Twelve Tables

spqr_laws.b: Any Roman citizen facing execution or corporal punishment may appeal to the popular assembly. No magistrate possesses absolute power over a citizen's life without this process.
spqr_laws.t: Provocatio ad Populum — Right of Appeal

spqr_laws.b: The tribunes of the plebs are sacrosanct. Their persons are inviolable by law and sacred oath. Any who raise a hand against a tribune may be killed by any citizen without penalty.
Decisions of the plebeian assembly (plebiscita) bind all Roman citizens equally. The plebs are not subjects of the patricians — they are Rome, and their voice carries the force of law.
spqr_laws.t: Lex Hortensia (287 BC) — Sovereignty of the Plebs

spqr_laws.b: Every magistracy has two or more holders of equal power who may veto each other. The Republic is built on the prevention of sole rule. No Roman magistrate governs alone.
spqr_laws.t: Collegium — The Principle of Joint Magistracy

spqr_laws.b: All magistracies last exactly one year. Re-election to the same office is forbidden for one year. This rule exists solely to prevent the accumulation of power that breeds tyranny.
War may only be declared by the Senate and ratified by the Comitia Centuriata. Unauthorised military action by any magistrate is a capital offence against the Republic.
spqr_laws.t: De Bello et Pace — On War and Peace

spqr_laws.b: Military command (imperium) is granted by the Senate for specific campaigns only. A general must surrender his imperium upon returning to Rome. He who enters the city in arms is an enemy of the Republic.
spqr_laws.t: Lex de Imperio — The Grant of Command

candidates.name: Fabius Minimus
candidates.speech:
Respected members of the senate. 

As im sure you’re aware, Hannibal has broken through the gates of Italy. The scales of war have been definitively tipped and Rome hangs in the balance. New Consuls must be elected, roles many are eagerly vying for.

And I too like many others, would also like to put my application forward

My military exploits however are limited to say the least, and im by most accounts a mediocre tactician. But I am extremely open to a savvy tactician getting elected as co-consul, to make up for my shortcomings in military theory.

Rome above class

Some now ask whether such a man serves Rome or only himself.".

spqr_parties.name: Optimates
spqr_parties.history: Founded by Publius Cornelius Scipio,Quintus Fabius Numerius and Lucius Valcarius Velor
spqr_parties.platform: The Optimates stands for military confrontation,economic progression and welfare of the common people of Rome.
spqr_parties.description: Political party wich stands for the people,by the people and of the people of Roma and for the Supreme prominence of Rome.

spqr_parties.name: Lex Aeterna
spqr_parties.platform: Our Focus lies on the upholding and strengthening of the Laws of the Republic. It stems from the believe that no one is supposed to stand above the law for it and our customs are the things that differentiate us from the Barbarians across our borders.
spqr_parties.description: Lex Aeterna is a Party founded by Aulus Domitius Ahenobarbus. It focuses on decisions around the laws and customes of the City of Rome and the Republic as a whole.

spqr_parties.name: Vox Populi Romani
spqr_parties.history: A reformed party after a chilling relevation of a traitor of Rome. Vox Populi Romani has reformed and purged its members to include only the most loyal senators of Rome. Founded by the following Senators, Nero Avitus Corvus, Benedictus Vopiscus Cassanus, Gaius Maenius Gaius Pictus Perictus, Gaius Ursulus Tertius, Maximus Valerius Altus, Quintus Velcha, Adeodatus Pistorius, Titus Flarminius Vercus
spqr_parties.platform: Vox Romani Populi believes the survival of the Roman Republic depends equally on a strong citizen body and a disciplined military. Rome must defend itself from external enemies while preserving justice, stability, and prosperity for its people.
spqr_parties.description: A party dedicated to creating a strong Rome. Protecting Rome both using its both its civil & military arms.

spqr_cemetery.cause: Died in service of the Republic

His body was discovered with several stab wounds in the Aventine, believed to have been killed last night after being caught up in a gang fight. Around him were a dozen men, some free, some slaves, several of whom had gold coins among their belongings, according to witnesses of those who looted them.

spqr_cemetery.bio:
Gaius Flaminius is a Roman statesman, general and former Consul known for his military experience, practical leadership and support among the plebeian population of the Republic. Though not born into one of Rome’s oldest aristocratic families, Flaminius rose through public service and political ability, first gaining prominence as Tribune of the Plebs. His support for Roman settlers and ordinary citizens earned him popularity throughout Italy while also bringing him into conflict with conservative senators.

As Consul, Flaminius led Roman armies against the Gallic tribes in northern Italy and achieved important victories that strengthened Roman control over the region. Later, as Censor, he oversaw the construction of the Via Flaminia, improving military movement, trade and communication between Rome and the north. Following the disaster at Trebia, Flaminius once again stepped forward as a leading political and military figure, advocating the rapid rebuilding of Rome’s armies and decisive action against Hannibal Barca.
spqr_cemetery.cause: Died in service of the Republic

He died after visiting the Tarpeian Rock, reportedly to admire the famous place where Rome throws its traitors.

According to witnesses, he leaned too far over the edge to “better understand the view,” slipped, and fell to his death.
Killed after a mule cart lost control in the crowded streets of Rome.
Crushed when a poorly built apartment block suddenly collapsed during the night.
Drowned after slipping in the baths, hitting his head, and being found too late by the attendants.
Deserted Rome hoping Hannibal would welcome him, but was mistaken for a spy by Carthaginian scouts and killed before reaching camp

spqr_force_types.name: Roman Legion
spqr_force_types.note: Standard Roman citizen legion. Reliable and politically central.

spqr_force_types.name: Socii Legion
spqr_force_types.note: Same manpower as a Roman legion, but half cost/upkeep. Requires careful Socii relations and political management.

spqr_force_types.name: Levy Legion
spqr_force_types.note: 20% cheaper gold cost/upkeep than a proper legion, same food burden. Lower quality until battle hardened.

spqr_force_types.name: Cisalpine Gallic Auxiliaries
spqr_force_types.note: Roughly 16% of a legion cost due to smaller size, with an added discount. Cheap but potentially unreliable.

spqr_force_types.name: Cisalpine Gallic Alae
spqr_force_types.note: Quarter-strength cavalry, 20% cheaper than proportional Socii Equites. Fast, cheap, but reliability is uncertain.

spqr_force_types.name: Trireme
spqr_force_types.note: Each trireme requires 200 crew/soldiers and counts toward total manpower.
